skill.name: ai-agents-architect
skill.description: Designs autonomous AI agent systems with tool use, memory, planning strategies, evaluation, and multi-agent orchestration.
skill.summary: Plan reliable AI agent architectures with controlled autonomy, tools, memory, and evaluation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command execution findings are false positives from markdown code fences and inline backticks in documentation. Static weak cryptography findings are false positives from ordinary prose about agent design, with no cryptographic APIs or executable code found. No evidence found of prompt injection, data exfiltration, filesystem access, network access, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer classified markdown code fences and inline related-skill names as Ruby shell backtick execution. These locations contain documentation bullets and skill names only, not executable Ruby or shell command invocation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible markdown fences and inline formatting. I found no Ruby source, shell syntax, command invocation, or user input path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Text Match
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns in normal prose about agent design and failure modes. No cryptographic API, hashing algorithm, cipher, password handling, or secret handling appears in these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations contain descriptive text such as agent architecture and tool descriptions. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic usage.

content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to balance autonomous behavior with clear control points. This skill helps structure agents with tools, memory, planning, evaluation, and failure handling.
actual_capabilities: Designs agent architectures for autonomous task execution.
Defines tool and function-calling strategies for agents.
Plans memory systems with selective storage and retrieval.
Compares ReAct and plan-and-execute control patterns.
Guides multi-agent orchestration and role boundaries.
Identifies evaluation, debugging, tracing, and failure-mode needs.
limitations: Does not provide runnable agent framework code by itself.
Requires LLM API access and function-calling knowledge.
Does not validate production security controls automatically.
May need adaptation for specific model providers or tool APIs.

use_cases.title: Plan a Customer Support Agent
use_cases.description: Design a support agent that can select tools, escalate safely, and preserve useful conversation memory.

use_cases.title: Review an Agent Architecture
use_cases.description: Assess an existing agent design for loops, unclear tools, missing tracing, and excessive autonomy.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Specialized Agents
use_cases.description: Define agent roles, handoff rules, and evaluation points for a multi-agent workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Agent Blueprint
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a basic AI agent for [task]. Include goals, tools, memory needs, stop conditions, and when it should ask for help.

prompt_templates.title: Tool Registry Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Defining function calling and tool selection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tool registry plan for an agent that performs [workflow]. Include tool schemas, selection rules, error handling, and usage limits.

prompt_templates.title: Agent Failure Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this agent design for likely failure modes: [design]. Focus on loops, tool overload, memory bloat, tracing, and unclear escalation.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Multi-Agent Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Coordinating several agents in one system
prompt_templates.prompt: Architect a multi-agent system for [business process]. Define roles, planning flow, shared memory, handoffs, evaluation metrics, and operator controls.

output_examples.input: Design an agent that researches competitors and drafts a weekly summary.
output: Recommended tools for search, document retrieval, and report drafting.
Memory policy for reusable company and competitor facts.
Iteration limits, escalation triggers, and evaluation checks.

output_examples.input: Review a plan that gives one agent twenty tools and no iteration limit.
output: Risk assessment for unlimited autonomy and tool overload.
Recommended tool grouping and task-specific tool exposure.
Required stop conditions, trace logging, and error surfacing.

output_examples.input: Create a multi-agent design for sales lead enrichment.
output: Separated planner, researcher, verifier, and writer responsibilities.
Handoff rules for incomplete or conflicting lead data.
Shared memory boundaries and quality review checkpoints.

best_practices: Set explicit iteration limits and escalation rules for every autonomous loop.
Expose only the tools needed for the current task and define each tool clearly.
Log agent decisions, tool calls, errors, and memory updates for review.
anti_patterns: Giving an agent unlimited autonomy without stop conditions.
Adding many overlapping tools before defining selection rules.
Using multiple agents when one well-scoped agent would be simpler.

faq.question: What does this skill help design?
faq.answer: It helps design AI agents with tools, memory, planning loops, evaluation, and multi-agent coordination.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and design review, not a complete production implementation.

faq.question: Which agent patterns does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers ReAct loops, plan-and-execute flows, tool registries, memory design, and orchestration patterns.

faq.question: Can it help prevent runaway agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes iteration limits, failure modes, escalation rules, and clear oversight points.

faq.question: Is this useful for multi-agent systems?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps define roles, handoffs, shared memory boundaries, and evaluation points.

faq.question: What knowledge should users already have?
faq.answer: Users should understand LLM API usage, function calling, and basic prompt engineering.
